Text: Did you solve today's lead code, BOTD?
If not, let's solve it together.
We are checking if an array is trionic.
To understand this, imagine the array as a sequence of movements
that must follow three strict rules.
It must go up, then down, then up again.
We solve this by identifying two transition points,
the peak and the valley.
The first rule is the initial climb.
We start at the beginning of the array
and move forward as long as the next number
is strictly larger than the current one.
This journey stops at the first peak.
For the array to be valid, this peak cannot be
at the very first position.
The array must have actually increased at least once.
The second rule is the final climb.
Instead of moving forward, we look at the array
from the end and move backward.
We continue as long as the number to the left
is strictly smaller.
This point where the increase from the right stops
is our valley.
Just like the peak, the valley cannot be
at the very last position.
There must be a clear increase at the tail end of the array.
The third and final rule is the middle bridge.
For the array to be trionic, the peak must occur before the valley.
More importantly, every single number between the peak
and the valley must be strictly decreasing.
This means every value in this middle section
must be smaller than the one that came before it.
If you find any part that stays the same
or moves upward in this middle gap, the array fails.
When an array successfully rises to a peak,
falls to a valley and then rises again to the finish,
it is trionic.
By checking these three movements, start climb,
middle fall and end climb,
we can determine the answer in a single pass.
Problem solved.
